This paper presents reduction of JWST imaging, photometric procedures, and SED-fitting methodology for 627.1 arcmin² across two JWST/PRIMER fields.
AI.result: 50-band photometric catalog spanning CFHT/U to JWST/MIRI F1800W covering 627.1 arcmin². Inclusion of deblended ground-based photometry improves photometric redshift accuracy by ~40% and reduces outlier fraction by ~60% compared to HST+JWST-only photometry.
AI.conclusion: The multi-wavelength catalog provides a key reference for statistical studies of galaxy formation and evolution in the early universe, with all catalogs and JWST mosaics to be made publicly available.

title: GASTON-GP: Source catalogue and millimetre variability of massive protostellar objects
comment: Accepted by MNRAS
summary: The processes governing protostellar mass growth remain debated, although episodic accretion is now understood as a key feature of protostellar evolution across all masses. Luminosity bursts have been observed in both low- and high-mass protostars, but the overall statistics remain limited, especially for high-mass objects. Over the past decade, numerical simulations of high-mass core collapse have provided a theoretical framework for interpreting protostellar variability, yet additional observational constraints are required to determine the characteristics and importance of bursts. In this work, we analyse data from GASTON-GP programme, which mapped a 2.4 square degrees region of the Galactic plane (centred at l = 24 deg) at 1.15 and 2.00 mm using NIKA2 on the IRAM 30 m telescope. The survey obtained 11 epochs over four years, offering the first opportunity to study millimetre variability in a large sample of massive protostellar sources. From the combined dataset, we constructed catalogues of 2925 compact sources at 1.15 mm and 1713 at 2.00 mm. Using a dedicated relative calibration scheme, we generated millimetre light curves for around 200 high-signal-to-noise sources and identified one variable candidate. However, it is not protostellar. Consequently, we report no robust detections of variable protostellar sources in GASTON field. This is the direct consequence of observational limitations (i.e., sensitivity, resolution) combined with the lack of any 100-fold luminosity bursts during the observations, which is consistent with estimates inferred from isolated core collapse simulations. This study highlights the need for future high-resolution, high-cadence surveys to constrain the accretion histories of massive protostars.

AI.tldr: No protostellar variability detected in GASTON-GP survey despite multi-epoch millimeter observations of massive protostars, highlighting observational limitations and need for future high-resolution surveys.
AI.motivation: To constrain episodic accretion in massive protostars by studying millimeter variability, as current statistics are limited despite theoretical predictions from simulations.
AI.method: Analyzed GASTON-GP survey data: 11 epochs over 4 years mapping 2.4 square degrees at 1.15mm and 2.00mm using NIKA2 on IRAM 30m telescope, with dedicated relative calibration for light curves.
AI.result: No robust detections of variable protostellar sources; only one variable candidate identified but not protostellar. Results consistent with simulations predicting rare 100-fold luminosity bursts.
AI.conclusion: Observational limitations (sensitivity, resolution) combined with rarity of extreme bursts prevented detection, highlighting need for future high-resolution, high-cadence surveys to constrain massive protostellar accretion.

title: Surprising increase of electron temperature in metal-rich star-forming region
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures, submitted to ApJL. Comments are welcome
summary: The electron temperature is a crucial parameter for the determination of the gas-phase metallicity of galaxies. Low electron temperature is expected for metal-rich galaxies, theoretically. We report the discovery that temperature, as measured through auroral-to-strong line ratios of O$^+$, trends in reverse directions at 12+log(O/H) $\geq$ 8.7. This trend remains consistent regardless of the emission line fitting method employed and is not attributable to contamination or dust attenuation correction. Notably, this phenomenon is not observed in other low-ionization ions, such as S$^+$ and N$^+$, which also probe electron temperature. The results are verified in two independent datasets. We analyze the potential cause for the high [OII] auroral-to-strong line ratios at high metallicities, finding that no specific reason could account for that. This finding challenges the fundamental principles of the direct $T_e$ method for metallicity measurement, warranting further investigation into its physical interpretation.

AI.tldr: Discovery of anomalous temperature trend in metal-rich galaxies: O⁺ auroral-to-strong line ratios increase with metallicity above 12+log(O/H) ≥ 8.7, contradicting theoretical expectations and not observed in other low-ionization ions.
AI.motivation: Electron temperature is crucial for determining gas-phase metallicity in galaxies. Theoretical models predict low electron temperatures for metal-rich galaxies, but observational verification of this relationship is needed.
AI.method: Analysis of auroral-to-strong line ratios of O⁺ in galaxies, using two independent datasets. Comparison with other low-ionization ions (S⁺ and N⁺) that also probe electron temperature. Verification through different emission line fitting methods and assessment of potential contamination/dust attenuation effects.
AI.result: Discovery of reverse temperature trend: O⁺ auroral-to-strong line ratios increase with metallicity at 12+log(O/H) ≥ 8.7, contrary to theoretical expectations. This phenomenon is specific to O⁺ and not observed in S⁺ or N⁺. The trend persists regardless of fitting method and is not attributable to contamination or dust correction.
AI.conclusion: The anomalous O⁺ temperature trend at high metallicities challenges fundamental principles of the direct Te method for metallicity measurement. No specific physical explanation was found for the high [OII] auroral-to-strong line ratios, warranting further investigation into the physical interpretation of this phenomenon.

title: A FAST Survey of H I Absorption in Low-power Radio Sources
comment: 20 pages, 8 figures, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We conducted a HI 21cm absorption study of a sample of 147 nearby (z < 0.1) low-power radio sources with $10\,\mathrm{mJy} < S_{1.4\,\mathrm{GHz}} < 30\,\mathrm{mJy}$ and $\log(P_{1.4\,\mathrm{GHz}}/\mathrm{W\,Hz^{-1}}) = 20.5-23.7$, using the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope. By investigating the origin and kinematics of HI absorbing gas, we aim to study the interplay between the active galactic nucleus (AGN) and its surrounding interstellar medium. Our observations detect 12 new absorbers, combining results from the pilot survey (three absorbers out of 26 sources), yielding a detection rate of $\sim10.2^{+3.1}_{-2.0}\%$. The detection rate in our sample is lower than in higher-power samples, which is likely due to emission dilution and the dominance of extended sources, indicating a gas-rich and star-forming-dominated population in low-power sources. Among new detections, most line profiles are narrow and show velocities close to systemic ones, consistent with rotating disks, while four show disturbed kinematics indicative of inflows or outflows. The fraction of outflow candidates rises with radio power, while the fraction of inflow ones remains constant, suggesting the effect of radio emission on driving HI outflows. In our sample, compact sources show a higher HI detection rate than extended sources. Contrary to expectations from higher-power samples, MIR-bright sources at low-power radio do not exhibit a higher HI detection rate or more disturbed kinematics. In low-power radio sources, blueshifted absorption occurs only in Seyferts and low-ionization nuclear emitting regions, indicating the connection between atomic outflows and the ionization state of AGN.

AI.tldr: HI 21cm absorption study of 147 low-power radio sources using FAST reveals 12 new absorbers, showing lower detection rates than high-power samples due to emission dilution and extended sources. Most profiles indicate rotating disks, while some show disturbed kinematics suggesting inflows/outflows, with outflow fraction increasing with radio power.
AI.motivation: To investigate the origin and kinematics of HI absorbing gas in low-power radio sources and study the interplay between AGN activity and the surrounding interstellar medium, particularly in the low-power regime where previous studies have been limited.
AI.method: Used the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope (FAST) to conduct HI 21cm absorption observations of 147 nearby (z < 0.1) low-power radio sources with flux densities 10-30 mJy at 1.4 GHz and radio powers log(P₁.₄GHz) = 20.5-23.7 W Hz⁻¹. Combined results with pilot survey data (3 absorbers from 26 sources).
AI.result: Detected 12 new absorbers, yielding a total detection rate of ~10.2% (with uncertainties). Detection rate lower than in higher-power samples, attributed to emission dilution and dominance of extended sources. Most line profiles are narrow with velocities near systemic, consistent with rotating disks. Four show disturbed kinematics indicating inflows/outflows. Outflow fraction increases with radio power while inflow fraction remains constant. Compact sources show higher HI detection than extended sources. MIR-bright sources don't show expected higher detection rates. Blueshifted absorption only occurs in Seyferts and LINERs.
AI.conclusion: Low-power radio sources represent a gas-rich, star-forming-dominated population with different HI absorption properties than high-power sources. Radio emission appears to drive HI outflows, with outflow fraction increasing with radio power. The connection between atomic outflows and AGN ionization state is evident, with blueshifted absorption exclusive to Seyferts and LINERs. Compact morphology favors HI detection in this low-power regime.
